title: 9 Best Herbal Teas For Dry Mouth
intro: Herbal teas for Dry mouth are a natural remedy that involves drinking infusions made from various herbs to alleviate symptoms of dry mouth, also known as xerostomia. This condition occurs when the salivary glands do not produce enough saliva, leading to dryness and discomfort in the mouth. Herbal teas can treat dry mouth due to their ability to stimulate saliva production, soothe irritated tissues, and provide anti-inflammatory properties. Examples of herbal teas that can help alleviate dry mouth symptoms include peppermint tea, which stimulates saliva production and provides a cooling sensation; ginger tea, which has anti-inflammatory properties that reduce swelling and pain; licorice root tea, which soothes and protects the mucous membranes; and slippery elm tea, which forms a protective barrier on the mucous membranes to prevent further irritation.

preparations.herb_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas have been traditionally used to treat dry mouth ailment, also known as xerostomia, due to its properties as a natural expectorant and anti-inflammatory agent. This herbal preparation helps to treat dry mouth by stimulating saliva production and reducing inflammation in the oral cavity. The bioactive constituents, such as glycyrrhizin and flavonoids, in Glycyrrhiza glabra teas have been shown to have a positive effect on the saliva glands, promoting the production of saliva and alleviating dry mouth symptoms. The benefits of using Glycyrrhiza glabra teas to treat dry mouth include its non-invasive and non-addictive nature, making it a safe and effective herbal remedy for individuals suffering from this condition.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 teaspoon of dried Glycyrrhiza glabra root.
Measure 1 cup of water in a pot and bring to boil.
Add the dried root to the boiling water and reduce heat.
Simmer for 5-7 minutes then strain the tea into a cup.
Drink the tea while warm, 2-3 times a day to soothe dry mouth.
preparations.preparation_study: According to the study mentioned, Glycyrrhiza glabra teas for dry mouth may help prevent gingival and periodontal diseases due to its anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antibacterial properties.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Glycyrrhiza glabra teas can be used to treat dry mouth ailments, but possible side effects may include increased blood pressure, low potassium levels, and fluid retention due to its licorice root content. To minimize potential side effects, it is recommended to limit the consumption of Glycyrrhiza glabra teas to moderate amounts (about 1-2 cups per day) and to gradually discontinue use if any adverse reactions occur.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Glycyrrhiza glabra tea, 2-3 times a day.
Use 1-2 teaspoons of dried roots per 8 oz of boiling water.
Steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the tea.
Gargle with the tea for 30 seconds to soothe the mouth.
Repeat as needed, but not exceeding 3 cups per day.

preparations.herb_desc: Echinacea purpurea teas have been traditionally used to treat dry mouth, also known as xerostomia, due to their anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents of Echinacea purpurea, including alkylamides, caffeic acid, and chicoric acid, help to stimulate saliva production and reduce inflammation in the mouth, thereby alleviating dry mouth symptoms. Additionally, Echinacea purpurea teas contain immunomodulatory compounds that help to regulate the immune system and prevent infections that can exacerbate dry mouth. The benefits of Echinacea purpurea teas in treating dry mouth include reduced symptoms, improved oral health, and enhanced overall well-being.
preparation_procedure: Gather 2 teaspoons of dried Echinacea purpurea flowers.
Boil 1 cup of water in a pot.
Pour the boiling water over the Echinacea flowers in a cup.
Let it steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the tea.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help with dry mouth.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Echinacea purpurea teas can be used to treat dry mouth ailments, but possible side effects may include digestive issues, allergic reactions, and interactions with certain medications, such as blood thinners and diabetes medications. To avoid adverse effects, one should be cautious of consuming large amounts of Echinacea purpurea teas, especially if experiencing gastrointestinal problems, and consider starting with a low dose to assess tolerance.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Echinacea purpurea tea 2-3 times a day.
Start with a small dose of 1 teaspoon of dried root per cup.
Gradually increase the dose to 2-3 teaspoons per cup as needed.
Use Echinacea purpurea tea for 7-10 days to help manage dry mouth.
Consult a healthcare professional before using Echinacea purpurea for long-term dry mouth relief.

preparations.herb_desc: Melissa officinalis teas, also known as lemon balm tea, have been used to treat the dry mouth ailment, particularly in individuals undergoing radiation therapy for head and neck cancer. This herbal preparation helps to treat dry mouth by reducing inflammation and promoting saliva production, thus alleviating symptoms of dryness and discomfort. The bioactive constituents of Melissa officinalis teas, including rosmarinic acid, apigenin, and linalool, contribute to their therapeutic effects, acting as antioxidants and anti-inflammatory agents to soothe and protect the oral mucosa. The benefits of using Melissa officinalis teas to treat dry mouth include improved oral health, reduced pain and discomfort, and enhanced quality of life for individuals experiencing this common side effect of cancer treatment.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 tablespoon of dried Melissa officinalis leaves.
Pour 1 cup of boiling water over the dried leaves in a cup.
Steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the mixture.
Add honey to taste, if desired, and stir well.
Drink the tea warm or at room temperature to soothe dry mouth.
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Integrative cancer therapies", Melissa officinalis teas for dry mouth were shown to significantly improve the salivary flow rate and reduce the severity of dry mouth in cancer patients.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Melissa officinalis teas can be used to treat dry mouth, but possible side effects may include gastrointestinal issues, such as nausea, and allergic reactions, such as skin rashes or itching. Precautions to take when using Melissa officinalis teas include monitoring your body's reaction and reducing the dosage if you experience any side effects, also being cautious with the interaction with other medications, especially blood thinners.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Melissa officinalis tea, 2-3 times a day.
Use 2-3 teaspoons of dried Melissa officinalis per cup of boiling water.
Take 1-2 teaspoons of Melissa officinalis tincture, 3-4 times a day.
Consume 1-2 cups of Melissa officinalis tea, before bedtime to improve sleep.
Monitor your dry mouth condition and adjust the dosage and frequency as needed.

preparations.herb_desc: Zingiber officinale teas, also known as ginger tea, have been traditionally used to treat dry mouth ailments due to their anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The bioactive constituents present in ginger tea, such as gingerols and shogaols, help to stimulate saliva production and reduce inflammation in the mouth, thereby alleviating dry mouth symptoms. The benefits of using ginger tea to treat dry mouth include its ability to soothe and calm the mouth and throat, reducing discomfort and pain associated with dry mouth. Regular consumption of ginger tea has been shown to provide long-term relief from dry mouth symptoms and promote overall oral health.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 teaspoon of dried Zingiber officinale root and 1 cup of boiling water.
Add the Zingiber officinale root to a cup and pour in the boiling water.
Let the mixture steep for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors to release.
Strain the tea into another cup to remove the Zingiber officinale root.
Drink the tea while it's warm to help soothe dry mouth.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Zingiber officinale teas can be beneficial in treating dry mouth ailments, but it may also cause side effects such as heartburn, nausea, and stomach discomfort due to the high ginger content. When using Zingiber officinale teas to treat dry mouth, take precautions to start with small doses and gradually increase as needed, and avoid consuming the tea on an empty stomach or before bedtime to minimize potential stomach upset.
preparation_usage: Drink 1 cup of Zingiber officinale tea, 2-3 times a day.
Start with 1/2 teaspoon of dried ginger per cup of hot water.
Gradually increase to 1 teaspoon if needed, as per your preference.
Use Zingiber officinale tea consistently for 7-10 days to see results.
Monitor your dry mouth condition and adjust frequency as needed.

preparations.herb_desc: Rosmarinus officinalis teas, also known as rosemary tea, have been traditionally used to treat dry mouth, a common symptom of various conditions such as Sjögren's syndrome and radiation-induced xerostomia. The anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties of this herbal preparation help to reduce inflammation and promote healing in the oral cavity, thereby alleviating dry mouth. The bioactive constituents of rosemary tea, including carnosic acid and rosmarinic acid, exhibit potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. Regular consumption of Rosmarinus officinalis teas has been found to promote saliva production, moisten the mouth, and provide relief from dry mouth symptoms, making it a potential natural remedy for this condition.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of fresh or dried Rosmarinus officinalis leaves.
Measure 1 tablespoon of the leaves and place them in a tea infuser or a heat-resistant cup.
Boil 1 cup of water and pour it over the leaves in the tea infuser or cup.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes, then strain the tea into another cup.
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help relieve dry mouth symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Rosmarinus officinalis teas can help alleviate dry mouth symptoms, but potential side effects may include stomach upset, diarrhea, and allergic reactions such as hives or itching. To minimize risks, users should take precautions like starting with small amounts, avoiding consumption before or after surgery, and being cautious when combining with other medications, especially blood thinners.
preparation_usage: Measure 1/2 cup of Rosmarinus officinalis tea leaves.
Steep the tea leaves in a cup of boiling water for 5 minutes.
Drink 1 cup of tea 2-3 times a day as needed for dry mouth.
Start with 1/2 cup and adjust dosage as needed for relief.
Continue using the tea for up to 7 days or as directed.

preparations.herb_desc: Cinchona officinalis teas have been traditionally used to treat dry mouth ailment due to their mucolytic and anti-inflammatory properties, which help to stimulate saliva production and reduce inflammation in the mouth. This herbal preparation contains bioactive constituents such as quinine, quinidine, and alkaloids, which have been shown to have a stimulating effect on the salivary glands, thereby alleviating dry mouth symptoms. The benefits of using Cinchona officinalis teas to treat dry mouth include improved oral health, reduced risk of tooth decay, and enhanced overall well-being. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis teas have been reported to have a soothing effect on the mucous membranes, providing relief from dry mouth discomfort and promoting a healthy oral environment.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 tablespoon of dried Cinchona officinalis bark
Measure 1 cup of boiling water in a teapot
Add the Cinchona officinalis bark to the boiling water and let it steep for 5-7 minutes
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the bark
Drink the tea 2-3 times a day to help soothe dry mouth
preparations.preparation_study: According to "Integrative cancer therapies", Cinchona officinalis teas for dry mouth were among the five herbal formulas shown to significantly improve the salivary flow rate compared to comparators.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Cinchona officinalis teas can be used to treat dry mouth, but possible side effects may include nausea, dizziness, and stomach upset due to the high quinine content, which may also interact with certain medications. Brew 1 teaspoon of Cinchona officinalis in 1 cup of boiling water.
Let the tea steep for 5-10 minutes, then strain it.
Drink 1-2 cups of Cinchona officinalis tea 2-3 times a day.
Maintain a consistent dosage and frequency for optimal results.

The herbal preparation helps to treat dry mouth by reducing inflammation and promoting saliva production, thereby alleviating the symptoms associated with this condition. Let the mixture steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the liquid using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth.
Add 1 tablespoon of honey to the tea, if desired, to help soothe a dry mouth.

preparations.herb_desc: Hydrastis canadensis teas, also known as goldenseal, have been traditionally used to treat dry mouth ailments due to their antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The berberine content in goldenseal helps to reduce inflammation and combat bacterial overgrowth in the mouth, promoting a healthy oral environment. The antiseptic and antibacterial properties of goldenseal's bioactive constituents, including berberine, hydrastine, and canadine, inhibit the growth of harmful microorganisms that can cause dry mouth. Regular consumption of goldenseal tea may help alleviate dry mouth symptoms, promote saliva production, and maintain overall oral health.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 teaspoon of dried Hydrastis canadensis root and a cup of boiling water.
Steep the root in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth.
Add honey to taste if needed. The recommended dose is 1 cup of tea, 2-3 times a day.
Drink the tea slowly to help soothe a dry mouth and promote saliva production.
preparations.preparation_study: N/A
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Hydrastis canadensis teas can be used to treat dry mouth, but it may cause side effects such as nausea, stomach upset, and diarrhea due to its bitter and astringent properties. To minimize potential side effects, it is recommended to start with small doses and gradually increase as needed, and to avoid using it for extended periods of time, as prolonged use can lead to liver and kidney damage.

preparations.herb_desc: Aloe vera teas have been traditionally used to treat dry mouth ailments due to their soothing and anti-inflammatory properties. The mucilage present in aloe vera teas helps to protect and lubricate the mucous membranes, reducing dryness and discomfort. The bioactive constituents such as aloin, aloe-emodin, and vitamins present in aloe vera teas stimulate saliva production and maintain oral health. Regular consumption of aloe vera teas has been shown to provide relief from dry mouth symptoms and promote overall oral well-being.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of water and 1 tablespoon of dried Aloe vera powder.
Boil the water in a pot.
Add the Aloe vera powder to the boiling water and stir.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and let it cool. Drink as needed to help soothe dry mouth.
preparations.preparation_study: According to the study, Aloe vera teas for dry mouth were not specifically mentioned, however, Aloe vera was one of the most frequently reported herbal compounds used to treat various oral diseases.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Aloe vera teas can help alleviate dry mouth symptoms, but they may cause some side effects such as gastrointestinal upset, diarrhea, and allergic reactions in rare cases. To use aloe vera teas safely, it's essential to start with small doses, monitor your body's reaction, and avoid consuming them if you have a history of digestive issues or are pregnant/breastfeeding, and also to store them in a cool, dry place to maintain their potency.

preparations.herb_desc: Curcuma longa teas have been used in traditional medicine to treat dry mouth, a common symptom of various health conditions, including xerostomia. The properties of this herbal preparation, including its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, help to treat dry mouth by reducing inflammation and promoting saliva production. The bioactive constituents of Curcuma longa, including curcumin and demethoxycurcumin, work together to inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines, thereby alleviating dry mouth symptoms. The benefits of using Curcuma longa teas to treat dry mouth include improved oral health, reduced risk of tooth decay and gum disease, and enhanced overall well-being.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 tsp of dried turmeric root (Curcuma longa) and 1 cup of boiling water.
Steep the turmeric root in the boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the liquid into a cup using a tea strainer or a piece of cheesecloth.
Add 1 tsp of honey (optional) to the turmeric tea to make it sweeter.
Drink the turmeric tea 2-3 times a day to help relieve dry mouth symptoms.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Journal of International Society of Preventive & Community Dentistry, Curcuma longa teas for dry mouth may not be directly mentioned in the study, but it can be inferred that Curcuma longa has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which can be beneficial in managing gingival and periodontal diseases, and its root is mentioned as an effective part in managing periodontal diseases.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Curcuma longa teas can be beneficial in treating dry mouth ailment, but potential side effects include gastrointestinal issues such as nausea, diarrhea, and stomach upset due to its strong turmeric content. To minimize these side effects, it is recommended to consume Curcuma longa teas in moderation, preferably with food, and to avoid taking it with other medications or substances that may interact with its active compounds.

preparations.herb_desc: Valeriana officinalis teas, also known as valerian root tea, have been traditionally used to treat dry mouth, a common symptom of various health conditions. The soothing and anti-inflammatory properties of this herbal preparation help to reduce inflammation and alleviate discomfort associated with dry mouth, making it easier to drink and swallow. The bioactive constituents of valerian root tea, including valerenic acid, valerenol, and valeranone, are responsible for its therapeutic effects, which help to stimulate saliva production and soothe the mucous membranes. The benefits of valerian root tea in treating dry mouth include promoting saliva flow, reducing inflammation, and providing relief from discomfort, making it a popular natural remedy for this condition.
preparation_procedure: Gather 1 cup of water and 1 teaspoon of dried Valeriana officinalis root.
Heat the water in a pot and bring it to a boil.
Reduce heat and add the dried root. Let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the mixture into a cup and discard the root.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if needed. Drink the tea warm.
preparations.preparation_study: According to Journal of dental hygiene : JDH, Valeriana officinalis teas for dry mouth are associated with xerostomia, which is a condition of dry mouth.
preparations.preparation_side_effect: Valeriana officinalis teas can be used to treat dry mouth, but possible side effects may include drowsiness, stomach upset, and interactions with other medications, such as sedatives or antidepressants. Precautions to take when using Valeriana officinalis teas include avoiding driving or operating heavy machinery, and being cautious when consuming alcohol or other substances that may enhance sedative effects.

preparation_study: According to Journal of International Society of Preventive & Community Dentistry, Glycyrrhiza glabra teas for dry mouth may be beneficial due to its antibacterial property, which can help maintain acid-alkali balance in saliva and prevent plaque and calculus formation, ultimately promoting oral health and potentially alleviating dry mouth symptoms.
intro_study: According to "Integrative cancer therapies", teas for dry mouth have potential benefits as herbal medicines were shown to significantly improve the salivary flow rate and reduce the severity of dry mouth in cancer patients.
